abstract: This paper examines how TikTok has become a major vector for mis- and disinformation about the Russian invasion of Ukraine, with particular attention to the role of sound as a platform-specific propaganda technique. Drawing on data from the first three months after the February 2022 invasion, the authors analyze how audio elements on TikTok contribute to the circulation of misleading war-related content. The study highlights sound as an under-examined but distinctive feature of TikTok's computational propaganda affordances.
key_claims: TikTok has emerged as a significant platform for the spread of mis- and disinformation related to the war in Ukraine.
Sound functions as a novel, platform-specific audiovisual technique for spreading disinformation, beyond traditional image- and text-based methods.
The scale of war-related content circulation on TikTok (e.g., 36.9 billion views under #Ukraine in three months) makes it a critical site for studying computational propaganda.
methods: Analysis of TikTok content posted in the first three months following Russia's February 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
Examination of videos circulating under war-related hashtags such as #Ukraine, including metrics like view counts.
Focus on sound as an analytical category alongside image and text in studying disinformation.
findings: Videos under the hashtag #Ukraine accumulated 36.9 billion views in the first three months after the invasion, with individual videos reaching up to 88 million views.
Sound on TikTok operates as a distinct audiovisual mechanism through which misleading information is conveyed and amplified.
contributions: Foregrounds sound as a previously underexplored modality of disinformation in platform studies.
Provides an empirical account of TikTok's role in the information environment surrounding the Ukraine war.
Extends the concept of computational propaganda to account for TikTok-specific affordances.
framing: The paper is positioned within research on computational propaganda, platform studies, and disinformation, and contributes to emerging scholarship on TikTok as a distinctive media environment. It expands prevailing text- and image-centric approaches to disinformation by foregrounding the sonic dimension of platform-specific propaganda.
